subject: Programming Principles and Practice with C and C++
subject_key: programming principles and practice with c and c++

topics.name: Computer Fundamentals
subtopics: Organization of a Computer (Von Neumann Architecture)
required_content: Explain what a program is and why programming languages exist
Describe the Von Neumann architecture — CPU, Memory, I/O — and how instructions flow
Explain the fetch-decode-execute cycle with a simple analogy
required_diagrams: Von Neumann architecture block diagram: CPU (ALU + CU + Registers), Memory Unit, Input/Output devices, connected by buses (Data Bus, Address Bus, Control Bus). Clean labeled diagram with arrows showing data flow.
common_mistakes: Confusing Von Neumann bottleneck with bus speed
Thinking CPU directly reads from secondary storage

topics.name: Basics of Computer Programming
subtopics: Classification of Programming Languages — MLL (Machine Level), ALL (Assembly Level), HLL (High Level)
Translators — Assembler: converts assembly to machine code (NASM example)
Translators — Compiler: translates entire HLL source to executable (GCC, batch process)
Translators — Interpreter: translates and executes HLL line-by-line (Python, runtime)
Compiler vs Interpreter — key differences in speed, error detection, portability
Programming Paradigms — Structured (top-down, functions, loops — C is the gold standard)
Programming Paradigms — Object-Oriented (objects, encapsulation, real-world modelling)
required_content: MLL: binary/hex instructions, CPU-specific, no portability. Example: 00011010 means ADD
ALL: mnemonics (MOV, ADD, JMP) replacing opcodes, 1-to-1 mapping, needs Assembler
HLL: English-like syntax, machine-independent, needs Compiler or Interpreter. Examples: C, Python, Java
Assembler: input=Assembly code, output=Object/Machine code. Example: NASM, GAS
Compiler: input=HLL source, output=Executable. Translates ALL at once. Example: GCC for C
Interpreter: input=HLL source, output=Direct execution. Line-by-line. Example: Python interpreter
Compiler advantage: faster execution. Interpreter advantage: easier debugging, platform flexibility
Unstructured: uses GOTO, hard to trace (spaghetti code), no modularity. Example: early BASIC
Structured: top-down decomposition, functions, loops, no GOTO. C is the model language
OOP: data + behaviour in objects, models real-world entities. Emerges from structured's scaling limits
required_diagrams: Language levels pyramid: 3-tier diagram. Bottom tier: Machine Code (binary 0s/1s, CPU-only). Middle tier: Assembly (mnemonics, architecture-specific). Top tier: High-Level Languages (C, Python, Java, human-readable). Arrows pointing upward labeled 'increasing abstraction'. Clean labeled diagram.

Compilation pipeline flowchart: Source Code (.c) → [Preprocessor] → Expanded Code → [Compiler] → Assembly (.s) → [Assembler] → Object Code (.o) → [Linker] → Executable (.exe). Linear left-to-right with labeled boxes and arrows showing file extensions at each stage.

Interpreter execution diagram: Source Code → [Interpreter] → line1 → Execute → line2 → Execute → ... → Result. Show line-by-line nature with loop arrow. Contrast with compiler one-shot translation above it.
required_tables: Comparison: Translator Type | Input | Output | Example || Assembler | Assembly code | Machine code | NASM || Compiler | HLL source | Executable | GCC || Interpreter | HLL source | Direct output | Python
common_mistakes: Thinking Java compiler produces native machine code (it produces bytecode)
Confusing assembler with compiler

Classification of Programming Languages.title: Classification of Programming Languages — MLL, ALL, HLL
Classification of Programming Languages.opening: Why does a classification of languages exist? Because as software complexity grew, writing in raw binary became impossible. Each generation of language traded hardware control for human productivity.
content_flow: MLL (1st Gen): Raw binary/hex. CPU executes directly. No translation needed. Example: 00111100 on 8085 = increment accumulator. Non-portable — Intel code won't run on ARM.
ALL (2nd Gen): Mnemonics replace binary opcodes. MOV instead of 10110000. 1-to-1 mapping with machine code. Needs Assembler to convert. Still architecture-specific.
HLL (3rd Gen): English-like syntax. sum = a + b. Machine-independent. Needs Compiler or Interpreter. Examples: C, Python, Java, C++.

Comparison: Language Level | Readability | Portability | Needs | Example || MLL | None | Zero | Nothing | 00111100 || ALL | Low | None | Assembler | MOV AX,BX || HLL | High | Full | Compiler/Interpreter | sum = a + b
Classification of Programming Languages.key_insight: As abstraction increases, programmer productivity increases but hardware control decreases. C sits at the sweet spot — HLL syntax with near-assembly performance.

Translators — Assembler: converts assemb.title: Translators — Assembler: Assembly → Machine Code
Translators — Assembler: converts assemb.opening: The CPU only understands 0s and 1s. Assembly is human-readable but not machine-readable. The Assembler is the bridge.
content_flow: What it does: Scans each assembly instruction and replaces it with its binary equivalent. Resolves symbolic labels like 'LOOP:' into actual memory addresses.
1-to-1 mapping: ONE assembly instruction → ONE machine instruction. This is what distinguishes it from a Compiler (which is 1-to-Many).
Input → Output: Assembly source (.asm) → Object file (.obj/.o) → then Linker produces executable.
Real assemblers: NASM (Netwide Assembler) and GAS (GNU Assembler) are used in OS development, device drivers, embedded firmware.
Common Mistake: Assembler ≠ Compiler. Assembler is for LOW-LEVEL (1:1). Compiler is for HIGH-LEVEL (1:Many).

Translators — Compiler: translates entir.title: Translators — Compiler: The Batch Translation Engine
Translators — Compiler: translates entir.opening: You write C code. The CPU can't read it. The Compiler translates the ENTIRE program before a single line executes.
content_flow: How it works: Reads ALL source code → Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis → Semantic Analysis → Code Generation → Optimized Executable (.exe or .out).
Key characteristic: Batch processing — ALL errors are reported together after scanning the full file. No partial execution.
Output is permanent: The compiled .exe can be run repeatedly without the source code or compiler being present.
GCC pipeline: gcc hello.c → preprocessor (.i) → compiler (.s) → assembler (.o) → linker → a.out
Speed advantage: Since translation happened once at compile time, execution is extremely fast. That's why C/C++ power OS kernels and game engines.

Translators — Interpreter: translates an.title: Translators — Interpreter: Line-by-Line Execution
Translators — Interpreter: translates an.opening: No compilation step. No executable file produced. The Interpreter reads your code and runs it immediately, one line at a time.
content_flow: How it works: Read line 1 → Check syntax → Translate to machine instruction → Execute → Move to line 2. Repeat.
Error behaviour: Stops IMMEDIATELY at the first error. The lines before it have already executed. The lines after it have not.
No output file: Translation is discarded after execution. Every run translates the code again — this is why interpreted programs are slower.
Where it's used: Python, JavaScript (in browsers), PHP, Ruby. Preferred for scripting, data science, and web backends where development speed matters more than runtime speed.
Interactive mode: Python's REPL is a live interpreter — type a line, see the result instantly. Ideal for learning and prototyping.

Compiler vs Interpreter — key difference.title: Compiler vs Interpreter — When to Use Which
Compiler vs Interpreter — key difference.opening: Both translate HLL to machine instructions. The difference is WHEN they do it and WHAT they produce. This decision shapes the entire character of a language.
content_flow: Comparison: Feature | Compiler | Interpreter || Translation timing | Before execution (once) | During execution (every run) || Error detection | All errors together | Stops at first error || Output | Executable file (.exe) | No permanent file || Execution speed | Fast (pre-translated) | Slower (translates at runtime) || Memory usage | Higher (stores object code) | Lower || Debugging ease | Harder (all errors at once) | Easier (see errors line by line) || Examples | C, C++, Rust, Go | Python, Ruby, JavaScript
The hybrid: Java compiles to bytecode (.class) — not machine code. The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) interprets the bytecode at runtime. This gives portability (any JVM runs it) with decent speed.
Industry reality: High-frequency trading, OS kernels, game engines → Compiled C/C++. Data science, web scripting, automation → Interpreted Python.
Compiler vs Interpreter — key difference.key_insight: Neither is universally better. Compiled = production performance. Interpreted = development flexibility.

Programming Paradigms — Unstructured (GO.title: Programming Paradigms — Unstructured: The GOTO Era
Programming Paradigms — Unstructured (GO.opening: Before structured programming existed, ALL programs were a single block of code. Repetition meant jumping back to earlier line numbers using GOTO. The result was chaos.
content_flow: How programs worked: 10 PRINT 'Hello' → 20 GOTO 10. Loops were implemented as backward jumps. Conditionals were IF x GOTO 50.
What goes wrong at scale: Imagine 2000 lines where any line can jump to any other line. Tracing execution becomes impossible. Changing one GOTO breaks ten others. This is called Spaghetti Code.
Real languages: Early BASIC and FORTRAN programs relied on line numbers and GOTO. Debugging them was a nightmare.
Why it still matters: GOTO still exists in C. Used correctly (error handling in system code) it's fine. Used carelessly, it recreates the spaghetti problem.
Common Mistake: Thinking 'unstructured' means random or incorrect logic. The program is logically valid — it's just unmaintainable as it grows.

Programming Paradigms — Structured (top-.title: Programming Paradigms — Structured: Discipline Through Functions
Programming Paradigms — Structured (top-.opening: Edsger Dijkstra's 1968 letter 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful' triggered a revolution. Programs should be organized into functions. Each function does ONE thing and returns. C was built on this philosophy.
content_flow: Core idea: Break the program into small, named, reusable functions. main() orchestrates — it calls printBill(), calculateTax(), sendEmail(). Each is independent and testable.
Three control structures replace GOTO: Sequence (execute in order), Selection (if-else, switch), Iteration (for, while, do-while). These three are sufficient for ANY program logic.
Top-down design: Start with the big problem. Decompose into sub-problems. Decompose further until each piece is simple enough to code in 10-20 lines.
C as the model: The Linux kernel (27 million lines) is pure structured C. main() calls thousands of functions. No class, no object — just functions calling functions.
What structured programming solved: Code becomes readable, testable, reusable. A function can be written by one developer and used by a hundred others.

Programming Paradigms — Object-Oriented.title: Programming Paradigms — OOP: When Structured Wasn't Enough
Programming Paradigms — Object-Oriented.opening: Structured programming conquered complexity up to a point. But as systems grew to millions of lines and hundreds of developers, functions alone weren't enough. OOP was the answer.
content_flow: The scaling problem: In a banking app, 50 functions all manipulate 'account data'. Any function can corrupt it. As the team grows, tracking who does what becomes impossible.

OOP's solution — Encapsulation: Bundle the data (balance, accountNo) and the functions that operate on it (deposit, withdraw) into a single unit called a Class. Only the class's own methods touch its data.
Key principles: Encapsulation (bundle + protect), Inheritance (reuse behaviour from parent class), Polymorphism (same method name, different behaviour per object).
C to C++: C++ was created by Bjarne Stroustrup in 1983 by adding 'Classes' to C. Structured C is the foundation — OOP is the extension for large-scale systems.
Where paradigms are used today: Linux kernel = Structured C. Chrome browser = OOP C++. Android apps = OOP Java/Kotlin. Data science scripts = Procedural Python.

topics.name: Inception of C
subtopics: History of Popular High-Level Languages — C, C++
required_content: C was developed by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs (1972) to rewrite UNIX
Timeline: BCPL → B → C → C++
Features: structured, portable, efficient, mid-level, rich library, extensible
required_diagrams: Language evolution timeline: BCPL (1967) → B (1969) → C (1972) → C++ (1985) → C99 → C11 → C17. Horizontal timeline with key milestones labeled.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | C | C++ || Paradigm | Procedural | Multi-paradigm || OOP Support | No | Yes || Standard Library | stdio.h etc | STL || Memory Mgmt | Manual | Manual + RAII
common_mistakes: Thinking C is outdated — it powers OS kernels, embedded systems, databases today

topics.name: Memory Organization of a Computer
subtopics: Reason for 2 types of memory — Primary & Secondary
SDD, HDD, RAM, Cache Memory, Registers
DRAM vs SRAM & RAM vs ROM
required_content: Explain the memory hierarchy: Registers > Cache > RAM > SSD/HDD
Why two types: speed vs capacity tradeoff
DRAM uses capacitors (slow, cheap, volatile), SRAM uses flip-flops (fast, expensive)
required_diagrams: Memory hierarchy pyramid diagram: at top — Registers (fastest, smallest), then Cache (L1/L2/L3), then RAM, then SSD, then HDD at bottom (slowest, largest). Each level labeled with speed and size. Use triangle/pyramid shape.
required_tables: Comparison: Memory Type | Speed | Capacity | Volatile | Cost || Registers | Fastest | Bytes | Yes | Highest || Cache | Very Fast | KBs-MBs | Yes | High || RAM | Fast | GBs | Yes | Medium || SSD | Medium | TBs | No | Low || HDD | Slow | TBs | No | Lowest
common_mistakes: Thinking RAM is permanent storage
Confusing cache memory with hard disk cache

topics.name: Introduction to Variables
subtopics: Declaration vs Initialization of a Variable
required_content: A variable is a named memory location that stores a value of a specific type
Declaration reserves memory, initialization assigns first value
Syntax: data_type variable_name = value;
required_diagrams: Memory map diagram showing variable storage: box labeled 'marks' at memory address 1000 containing value 95, box 'gpa' at 1004 containing 8.7, box 'grade' at 1008 containing 'A'. Show address, name, value for each.
common_mistakes: Using a variable before initializing it (garbage value)
Naming variables with spaces or starting with a digit

topics.name: Introduction to Functions
required_content: Functions promote code reuse, modularity, and readability
A function has: return type, name, parameters, body
Prototype declares the function before use; definition provides the implementation
required_diagrams: Function call stack diagram: main() frame at bottom, add(10,20) frame on top showing parameters a=10, b=20, return value 30 flowing back to main(). Labeled stack boxes.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the function prototype before main() causing implicit declaration warning
Confusing function declaration (prototype) with function definition

topics.name: main() Function in C
subtopics: Need of main() in our Programs
First C Program — Print Hello, World!
required_content: main() is the entry point of every C program — execution starts here
int return type signals success (0) or failure (non-zero) to the OS
common_mistakes: Using void main() instead of int main() — undefined behaviour in standard C
Forgetting #include <stdio.h> before using printf()

topics.name: Life Cycle of a C Program
subtopics: Execution Flow of a C Program
Different stages involved in the execution of a C Program
required_content: Stages: Source Code (.c) → Preprocessor → Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Executable
Each stage transforms the program into a closer-to-machine form
required_diagrams: C program lifecycle flowchart: Source Code (.c) → [Preprocessor] → Expanded Code → [Compiler] → Assembly Code (.s) → [Assembler] → Object Code (.o) → [Linker + Library Files] → Executable (.exe). Linear left-to-right flow with labeled boxes and arrows.
common_mistakes: Thinking compilation directly produces the executable — linker step is separate

topics.name: Compilation vs Interpretation
required_content: Header files (.h) contain declarations; Library files (.lib/.a) contain compiled implementations
Object file is partially compiled code; Executable is fully linked runnable code
required_tables: Comparison: Aspect | Compilation | Interpretation || Translation | Entire program at once | Line by line || Speed | Faster execution | Slower execution || Error Detection | All at compile time | At runtime || Example | C, C++ | Python, JavaScript
common_mistakes: Thinking header files contain actual function code — they only have declarations

topics.name: Tokens in C
subtopics: Tokens in C — Keywords, Identifiers, Literals, Operators, Comments, Separators, Preprocessor Directives
Rules for defining Identifiers in C
required_content: A token is the smallest meaningful unit in a C program
C has 32 keywords (C89), all lowercase, reserved — cannot be used as identifiers
Identifiers: user-defined names, must start with letter/underscore, case-sensitive
required_tables: Table: Token Type | Definition | Examples || Keyword | Reserved word | int, if, while, return || Identifier | User-defined name | main, sum, count || Literal | Fixed value | 42, 3.14, 'A', "hello" || Operator | Operation symbol | +, -, *, /, == || Comment | Non-executable text | // single, /* multi */ || Separator | Delimits tokens | ; , () {} []
common_mistakes: Using a keyword as a variable name (e.g., int int = 5 — compile error)
Thinking identifiers are case-insensitive (sum ≠ Sum ≠ SUM in C)

topics.name: I/O in C
required_content: printf() for output, scanf() for input — both use format specifiers
Format specifiers: %d (int), %f (float), %c (char), %s (string), %lf (double)
Escape sequences: \n (newline), \t (tab), \\ (backslash), \" (double quote)
required_tables: Table: Format Specifier | Data Type | Example || %d | int | printf("%d", 42) → 42 || %f | float | printf("%f", 3.14) → 3.140000 || %lf | double | printf("%lf", 3.14) || %c | char | printf("%c", 'A') → A || %s | string | printf("%s", "Hi") → Hi || %p | pointer | printf("%p", &x)
common_mistakes: Forgetting & before variable name in scanf() for primitive types
Using %d for float — causes garbage output

topics.name: Data Types in C
subtopics: Need for Data Types in a Programming Language
required_content: Data types tell the compiler: how much memory to allocate and how to interpret the bits
Primitive: int, float, double, char, _Bool. Non-primitive: arrays, pointers, structs, unions
required_diagrams: Data types classification tree: root 'C Data Types' → branches 'Primitive' (int, float, double, char, _Bool) and 'Derived' (array, pointer, function) and 'User-defined' (struct, union, enum). Tree diagram with labeled nodes.

topics.name: Integer Data Types
subtopics: Size Qualifiers: short, int, long, long long
How integer data is stored in Memory using Base-2 format
Different Suffixes associated with Integer Literals
Format specifiers for printing in different number systems
required_content: short (2 bytes), int (4 bytes), long (4/8 bytes), long long (8 bytes)
signed range: -2^(n-1) to 2^(n-1)-1. unsigned range: 0 to 2^n - 1
Binary storage: MSB is sign bit for signed types. Two's complement for negatives
required_tables: Table: Data Type | Size (bytes) | Signed Range | Unsigned Range || short | 2 | -32768 to 32767 | 0 to 65535 || int | 4 | -2^31 to 2^31-1 | 0 to 2^32-1 || long | 4/8 | platform-dependent | platform-dependent || long long | 8 | -2^63 to 2^63-1 | 0 to 2^64-1
common_mistakes: Assuming int is always 4 bytes — it's platform-dependent
Integer overflow wraps around silently — no exception in C

topics.name: Floating-point Data Types
subtopics: How real numbers are stored in Memory using IEEE-754 format
Precision & Range of floating-point data types
Need for long double Data Type
Different ways of expressing floating-point literals
required_content: float: 4 bytes, 6-7 decimal digits precision. double: 8 bytes, 15-16 digits precision
IEEE-754 format: 1 sign bit + 8 exponent bits + 23 mantissa bits (for float)
Default floating literal is double — use f suffix for float: 3.14f
required_tables: Table: Type | Size | Precision (digits) | Range || float | 4 bytes | 6-7 | ±3.4e-38 to ±3.4e+38 || double | 8 bytes | 15-16 | ±1.7e-308 to ±1.7e+308 || long double | 12/16 bytes | 18-19 | ±3.4e-4932 to ±1.1e+4932
common_mistakes: Using == to compare floats — floating-point arithmetic is imprecise
Forgetting the f suffix: float x = 3.14 assigns a double to a float (implicit cast)

topics.name: Character Data Type
subtopics: Different ways of initializing char variables
signed vs unsigned qualifiers with char data type
Different ways of scanning character input — scanf() vs getchar()
required_content: char is 1 byte. Stores ASCII code (0-127 standard, 0-255 extended)
ASCII: 'A'=65, 'a'=97, '0'=48. Arithmetic on chars is valid in C
signed char: -128 to 127. unsigned char: 0 to 255
required_tables: Table: Character | ASCII Value | Category || '0'-'9' | 48-57 | Digits || 'A'-'Z' | 65-90 | Uppercase || 'a'-'z' | 97-122 | Lowercase || ' ' | 32 | Space || '\n' | 10 | Newline || '\0' | 0 | Null terminator
common_mistakes: Using scanf("%c", &c) without leading space — reads leftover newline from previous input
Confusing '5' (char, ASCII 53) with 5 (integer)

topics.name: Type Casting in C
subtopics: Strongly-typed vs Weakly-typed | Statically-typed vs Dynamically-typed
required_content: Implicit: compiler automatically promotes lower type to higher (int → float)
Explicit: programmer uses (type) cast operator — (int)3.9 gives 3, not 4 (truncation)
Overflow wraps around: INT_MAX + 1 wraps to INT_MIN (undefined behaviour technically)
required_tables: Comparison: Aspect | Implicit Casting | Explicit Casting || Triggered by | Compiler automatically | Programmer using (type) || Direction | Lower → Higher type | Any direction || Risk | None (safe widening) | Data loss possible || Example | int x=5; float f=x; | int i=(int)3.9;
common_mistakes: Expecting (int)3.9 to give 4 — C truncates towards zero, does NOT round
Integer division: 7/2 = 3, not 3.5 — both operands must be float for float result

topics.name: Operators in C
subtopics: Behavior of Pre & Post in C
Bitwise Operators — AND, OR, XOR, NOT
Bitwise Shift Operators — Left Shift (<<), Right Shift (>>)
Problems on Bitwise Operators: Set/Unset/Flip/Check ith bit
required_content: Pre-increment (++i): increment first, then use. Post-increment (i++): use first, then increment
Bitwise AND (&), OR (|), XOR (^), NOT (~) operate on individual bits
Left shift (<<n) = multiply by 2^n. Right shift (>>n) = divide by 2^n
required_tables: Table: Operator | Type | Example | Result || ++ (pre) | Unary | ++a (a=5) | a becomes 6, returns 6 || ++ (post) | Unary | a++ (a=5) | returns 5, a becomes 6 || & | Bitwise AND | 5 & 3 | 1 (0101 & 0011) || | | Bitwise OR | 5 | 3 | 7 (0101 | 0011) || ^ | Bitwise XOR | 5 ^ 3 | 6 (0101 ^ 0011) || << | Left Shift | 2 << 3 | 16 (multiply by 8)
common_mistakes: Confusing & (bitwise AND) with && (logical AND)
Post-increment in expressions: int b = a++ + a++ is undefined behaviour in C

topics.name: Control Constructs in C
required_content: 3 categories: Conditional (if/switch), Iterative (for/while/do-while), Jump (break/continue/goto/return)
Ternary: condition ? expr_if_true : expr_if_false — compact if-else
Short-circuit: && stops at first false, || stops at first true
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | if-else | switch || Expression type | Any boolean | Integer/char/enum || Multiple conditions | if-else-if chain | Multiple case labels || Range check | ✓ Possible | ✗ Not directly || Fall-through | ✗ N/A | ✓ Without break || Readability | Better for ranges | Better for discrete values
common_mistakes: Forgetting break in switch — causes fall-through to next case
Using = instead of == in conditions: if(x = 5) always true (assigns 5)

topics.name: Loops in C
subtopics: Loops in C — for, while, do-while
Common example to demonstrate all 3 loops
Pattern Printing — Star, Number, Character Patterns
required_content: for: when iteration count is known. while: when condition-driven. do-while: executes at least once
Loop components: initialization, condition, update
Nested loops for pattern printing — outer loop = rows, inner loop = columns
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | for | while | do-while || Use case | Known iterations | Unknown, condition-based | Execute at least once || Entry/Exit | Entry-controlled | Entry-controlled | Exit-controlled || Syntax | for(init;cond;update) | while(cond) | do{...}while(cond) || Min executions | 0 | 0 | 1
common_mistakes: Off-by-one error: using < vs <= in loop condition
Infinite loop: forgetting to update loop variable inside while body

topics.name: Jump Statements in C
required_content: break: exits the innermost loop or switch immediately
continue: skips current iteration, moves to next
goto: unconditional jump to a label — generally avoided in structured programming
common_mistakes: Using break inside nested loops — only exits the innermost loop, not all loops
Goto creates spaghetti code — use structured alternatives

topics.name: Memory Management in C
subtopics: Different memory segments allocated for C program
required_content: A C program's memory is divided into: Code/Text, Data (initialized globals), BSS (uninitialized globals), Heap (dynamic), Stack (local vars + function calls)
Stack grows downward (LIFO), Heap grows upward (manual management)
required_diagrams: C program memory layout diagram (vertical rectangle representing RAM): Stack (top, grows downward) | ↕ Free Memory ↕ | Heap (grows upward) | BSS (uninitialized globals) | Data Segment (initialized globals) | Text/Code Segment (bottom). Clearly labeled sections with arrows showing growth direction.
common_mistakes: Confusing stack overflow (too deep recursion) with buffer overflow
Thinking global variables go on the stack — they go in Data/BSS segment

topics.name: Functions in C
subtopics: Types of Variables — Local, Global, Static, Extern, Register
required_content: 4 types: void→void (no param, no return), void→return, param→void, param→return
Pass-by-value: copy of variable passed — changes don't affect original
Pass-by-address: address passed via pointer — changes affect original
required_tables: Comparison: Variable Type | Scope | Lifetime | Storage || Local | Inside function | Function execution | Stack || Global | Entire program | Program lifetime | Data segment || Static (local) | Inside function | Program lifetime | Data segment || Extern | Across files | Program lifetime | Data segment || Register | Inside function | Function execution | CPU register (hint)
common_mistakes: Expecting pass-by-value to modify the original variable
Forgetting base case in recursion — causes stack overflow

topics.name: Arrays in C
required_content: Array: contiguous block of memory storing same-type elements, accessed by index (0-based)
2D array stored row-major in memory: a[0][0], a[0][1], a[1][0], a[1][1]...
Jagged arrays: array of pointers, each pointing to arrays of different lengths
required_diagrams: Memory map diagram for 2D array int a[2][3]: Show 6 contiguous memory boxes labeled a[0][0], a[0][1], a[0][2], a[1][0], a[1][1], a[1][2] with sequential addresses (e.g. 1000, 1004, 1008...). Show logical 2D grid on left and linear memory layout on right.
common_mistakes: Array index out of bounds — C does not check bounds, causes undefined behaviour
Confusing array name (pointer to first element) with the array itself

topics.name: Pointers in C
subtopics: Dynamic Memory Allocation — malloc(), calloc(), realloc(), free()
required_content: Pointer stores the memory address of another variable. Declared as: type *ptr;
& = address-of operator. * = dereference operator (get value at address)
malloc(n*sizeof(type)) allocates n bytes on heap — returns void*, must cast
required_diagrams: Pointer diagram: Two memory boxes — box labeled 'x' at address 1000 containing value 42, and box labeled 'ptr' at address 2000 containing value 1000 (the address of x). Arrow from ptr box pointing to x box. Label the arrow 'points to'.
required_tables: Table: Function | Purpose | Returns || malloc(size) | Allocates size bytes, uninitialized | void* or NULL || calloc(n, size) | Allocates n*size bytes, zero-initialized | void* or NULL || realloc(ptr, size) | Resize previously allocated block | void* or NULL || free(ptr) | Releases allocated memory | void
common_mistakes: Forgetting to free() heap memory — causes memory leak
Dereferencing NULL pointer — causes segmentation fault (program crash)

topics.name: Strings in C
subtopics: Strings in C — Different ways of handling strings
required_content: C has no built-in string type — strings are char arrays terminated by null character '\0'
String literal "hello" stored as {'h','e','l','l','o','\0'} — 6 bytes, not 5
string.h provides: strlen(), strcpy(), strcmp(), strcat(), strstr(), strtok()
required_tables: Table: Function | Header | Purpose | Example || strlen(s) | string.h | Length (excl \0) | strlen("Hi")=2 || strcpy(d,s) | string.h | Copy s into d | strcpy(dest, src) || strcat(d,s) | string.h | Append s to d | strcat(s1, s2) || strcmp(a,b) | string.h | Compare: 0 if equal | strcmp("a","a")=0 || strstr(s,sub) | string.h | Find substring | strstr("hello","ell")
common_mistakes: Using == to compare strings — compares pointers, not content. Use strcmp()
Buffer overflow: strcpy() does not check destination size — use strncpy() instead

topics.name: Preprocessor Directives in C
required_content: #define creates macros — text substitution before compilation, not a variable
Conditional compilation: #ifdef allows platform-specific or debug code blocks
typedef creates aliases: typedef unsigned long int ULONG; — improves readability
required_tables: Table: Directive | Purpose | Example || #include <file> | Include system header | #include <stdio.h> || #include "file" | Include local header | #include "mylib.h" || #define NAME val | Define macro constant | #define MAX 100 || #define F(x) expr | Function-like macro | #define SQ(x) (x*x) || #ifdef / #endif | Conditional compilation | #ifdef DEBUG...#endif || #undef | Remove macro definition | #undef MAX
common_mistakes: Adding semicolon after #define: #define PI 3.14; — expands to 3.14; causing syntax error
Missing parentheses in function-like macros: #define SQ(x) x*x → SQ(2+3) = 2+3*2+3 = 11, not 25

topics.name: Structures in C
subtopics: Structures in C — definition, syntax, members
Pointer to struct and arrow operator (->)
required_content: struct groups variables of different types under one name
Access members: struct_var.member. Via pointer: ptr->member (same as (*ptr).member)
Size of struct may include padding bytes for alignment
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | struct | union || Memory | Sum of all members | Size of largest member || Members active | All simultaneously | Only one at a time || Use case | Group related data | Memory-efficient variant storage || Example | Student {int,char,float} | Data {int i; float f;}
common_mistakes: Forgetting the semicolon after closing brace of struct definition
Using . operator with a struct pointer — must use -> or (*ptr).member

topics.name: Unions and Enums in C
subtopics: Unions in C — definition, memory sharing
enums in C — named integer constants
required_content: Union: all members share the same memory location — only one member valid at a time
enum assigns names to integer constants, starting from 0 by default
enum improves code readability: enum Direction {NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST};
common_mistakes: Reading a union member other than the last one written — undefined behaviour
Thinking enum values are strings — they are integers with names

topics.name: File Handling in C
subtopics: File Handling in C — opening, reading, writing, closing
File modes: r, w, a, rb, wb
required_content: FILE *fp = fopen("file.txt", "r") — returns NULL on failure
Always close files with fclose() to flush buffers and release resources
fseek(fp, offset, SEEK_SET/SEEK_CUR/SEEK_END) for random access
required_tables: Table: Mode | Meaning | File exists | File absent || r | Read only | Opens it | Returns NULL || w | Write only | Overwrites | Creates new || a | Append | Adds to end | Creates new || r+ | Read+Write | Opens it | Returns NULL || w+ | Read+Write | Overwrites | Creates new
common_mistakes: Not checking if fopen() returned NULL before using the file pointer
Forgetting fclose() — data may not be written (buffered I/O)

topics.name: C++ Fundamentals
subtopics: C Header files vs C++ Header files
STL — Vector, List, Stack, Queue, Map
required_content: C++ uses <iostream> instead of <stdio.h>. cout << and cin >> replace printf/scanf
namespace std:: prevents name collisions. using namespace std; imports all std names
STL containers: vector (dynamic array), stack (LIFO), queue (FIFO), map (key-value)
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | C | C++ || I/O | printf/scanf | cout/cin || Header | stdio.h | iostream || Namespace | N/A | std:: || OOP | No | Yes || String type | char array | std::string || Dynamic array | malloc/realloc | std::vector
common_mistakes: Forgetting using namespace std; and then getting 'cout was not declared' error
Mixing C-style printf with C++ cout — not wrong but inconsistent style
